brief:
The most striking feature of this week's curve is not what moved but what didn't — **zero basis points of change across every single tenor**, from the 91-day bill at **9.85%** to the 15-year bond at **12.68%**. A perfectly frozen curve in an environment of **9.13% CPI inflation**, a repo rate at **10%**, and excess liquidity sitting at a staggering **৳1.9 lakh crore** tells you one thing clearly: the market has priced in a prolonged policy pause and is now waiting for the next catalyst. The front end is anchored firmly by the call money rate at **9.80%**, just 20 basis points below repo, which means interbank liquidity is comfortable but not sloppy. What should draw your attention, however, is the curve's internal architecture. The 2-year bond at **11.95%** is trading **80 basis points above** the 10-year at **11.15%** and **113 basis points above** the 5-year at **10.82%** — a pronounced belly inversion that screams supply indigestion. The 15-year at **12.68%** sits a full **103 basis points above** the 20-year at **11.65%**, which is an outright kink that reflects either legacy issuance distortion or thin secondary liquidity in that bucket. This is not a normal upward-sloping sovereign curve; it is a curve with a dislocated belly, and that dislocation is where both risk and opportunity sit.

The structural forces behind this shape are not mysterious. Government revenue collection at **44.9% of the FY26 target** with barely four months remaining means the fiscal authority will need to lean heavily on domestic borrowing through the June fiscal year-end — the borrowing calendar will only get heavier from here. ADP implementation at a paltry **11.5%** suggests the spending has been back-loaded, which means fourth-quarter treasury bill and bond supply could surge precisely when banks are window-dressing for year-end. At the same time, total government debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** — **40% of GDP** — with interest-to-revenue already at **22%** puts a structural floor under long-end yields; the sovereign is not getting cheaper to fund anytime soon. On the demand side, an ADR of **75.8%** means banks have ample room to absorb government paper, which explains why T-bill auctions are clearing cleanly — the 91-day at **1.92x** bid-cover and the 364-day at **1.75x** both cleared with zero devolvement. But move out to the 10-year and the picture shifts: a bid-cover of only **1.4x** with **10% devolvement** on the March 8 auction tells you banks are reluctant to extend duration at **11.15%** when they can park money in the 2-year at **11.95%** for far less duration risk and 80 basis points more carry. That reluctance is rational, and it is what is keeping the belly inverted.

Here is the call. The 2-year at **11.95%** remains the most attractive point on this curve on a risk-adjusted basis — it offers **195 basis points** over repo, nearly **280 basis points** of real yield over headline CPI, and sits at the widest spread to any adjacent tenor. If you have SLR capacity to deploy, that is where you concentrate. I would be a disciplined buyer of 5-year paper at **10.82%** only on dips toward **11%** in secondary, given the clean **1.6x** bid-cover and zero devolvement at that tenor — it is the one point where the market is comfortable with duration. I would actively avoid the 10-year until devolvement clears and bid-cover improves above **1.6x**; catching a falling knife at **11.15%** when the belly pays you more is poor portfolio construction. On the short end, rolling 91-day bills at **9.85%** is essentially earning negative carry against the **10% repo** rate — it is a liquidity buffer, not an investment, and should be sized accordingly. The key catalyst over the next two weeks is whether Bangladesh Bank signals any adjustment to the corridor or CRR ahead of the fiscal year-end borrowing surge. If BB holds firm and the government front-loads issuance, expect the belly inversion to deepen further, pushing the 2-year toward **12%** or above — and that would be a generational entry point for fixed-income portfolios. If, conversely, BB hints at liquidity tightening to defend the taka with reserves at **$35.1 billion** and Brent at **$78.4**, the front end reprices first and fastest, and your short-dated book takes the hit. Position for the former, hedge for the latter.
